agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Integration of two previously independent bounded contexts — Warehouse Management (stock) and Sales/Order Management (orders) — to enforce stock sufficiency at order placement time and expose real-time stock visibility to the sales team. This is a classic cross-context coupling challenge in logistics and retail domains.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. Neither context currently owns this integration — a new anti-corruption layer or domain service is implied but unspecified.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"Warehouse context: the physical on-hand quantity of a Product in the warehouse. Integer >= 0. Reflects received goods minus picked goods."},{"term":"Product","definition":"Warehouse context: an item tracked for inventory purposes, identified by a Product ID."},{"term":"Order","definition":"Sales context: a customer's intent to purchase one or more products. Contains OrderLines."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"Sales context: a single line item within an Order, referencing a Product ID and a desired Quantity."},{"term":"Available Stock","definition":"NOT YET DEFINED — this is the dangerous term. Does it mean on-hand minus already-allocated? On-hand minus picked? On-hand minus all open orders? This term does not exist in either context today and must be explicitly defined before implementation."},{"term":"Blocked Order","definition":"NOT YET DEFINED — the feature says 'block it' but does not specify whether this means rejected, held for review, or queued pending restock. Each has radically different business semantics."},{"term":"Stock Reservation / Allocation","definition":"NOT YET DEFINED — if stock is checked at order time, does placing an order reserve (decrement available) stock? Without reservation, two simultaneous orders can both pass the check and cause oversell."},{"term":"Stock Available Indicator","definition":"NOT YET DEFINED — a real-time signal shown to the sales team. 'Real-time' in warehouse operations is a domain myth; stock changes with every pick, receive, adjustment, and cycle count."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel for any Product must never go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Negative stock represents a physical impossibility in the warehouse. If this is violated, the system's inventory data is corrupt and cannot be trusted for any downstream decision.","testable_assertion":"For all Products at all times: StockLevel >= 0."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An Order may contain multiple OrderLines, each referencing a Product ID and Quantity > 0.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"An OrderLine with zero or negative quantity is structurally invalid — it has no business meaning.","testable_assertion":"Every OrderLine has Quantity >= 1 and references a valid Product ID."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Stock sufficiency check at order placement must compare against a well-defined 'Available Stock' concept, not raw StockLevel.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy decision about how to calculate availability. The definition of 'available' (on-hand vs. on-hand minus allocated vs. ATP) is a policy that could change.","testable_assertion":"The system uses a single, documented formula for Available Stock, and all sufficiency checks use that formula consistently."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"If stock is checked at order time, concurrent orders for the same Product must not both succeed if combined Quantity exceeds Available Stock (no oversell).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Overselling creates fulfillment failures, customer disappointment, and operational chaos. If two orders can both pass the check and together exceed stock, the check is meaningless — the system state is logically inconsistent.","testable_assertion":"Given Available Stock = 5 for Product X, two simultaneous orders for Quantity 3 each must not both be accepted. At most one succeeds; the other is blocked."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The 'stock available' indicator shown to the sales team is eventually consistent and must not be treated as a guarantee of fulfillability.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a UX/policy decision. The indicator is informational. Real-time accuracy in warehouse contexts is impractical due to constant physical activity (picks, receives, adjustments, breakage, cycle counts). A VP could decide to show a traffic-light instead of a number.","testable_assertion":"The stock indicator displays data no older than a defined staleness threshold (e.g., N seconds), and the UI communicates that the value is approximate."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Two customers simultaneously order the last 5 units of a Product (each ordering 5).","expected_behavior":"Exactly one order succeeds; the other is blocked. The system must not oversell. This requires either pessimistic locking, optimistic concurrency with conflict detection, or a reservation/allocation mechanism."},{"scenario":"Stock is received (supplier delivery) while an order is being evaluated for sufficiency.","expected_behavior":"The sufficiency check must use a consistent snapshot. If stock arrives mid-check, the order should either see the pre-arrival or post-arrival state — not a torn read."},{"scenario":"A Product exists in Sales context but has never been stocked in Warehouse context (new product, no inventory record).","expected_behavior":"The system must handle missing stock records gracefully — likely treating Available Stock as 0 and blocking the order, not throwing an error."},{"scenario":"An Order contains 10 OrderLines across 7 Products; 6 Products have sufficient stock, 1 does not.","expected_behavior":"Business must decide: is the entire Order blocked (all-or-nothing), or only the insufficient line? Partial fulfillment vs. atomic order acceptance is a critical undefined policy."},{"scenario":"Stock indicator shows 'available' but by the time the sales rep submits the order, stock has been claimed by another order.","expected_behavior":"The order is blocked despite the indicator having shown availability. The UI must not imply that a green indicator guarantees order success."},{"scenario":"A customer places an order, it passes the stock check and is accepted, but before picking occurs a stock adjustment (damage, shrinkage) reduces stock below the committed amount.","expected_behavior":"Undefined in the spec. This reveals the need for a Reservation/Allocation concept — accepted orders should decrement Available Stock even before physical picking."},{"scenario":"The Warehouse system is temporarily unavailable when a customer places an order.","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Should the order be rejected (fail-closed), accepted optimistically (fail-open), or queued? Each choice has different business tradeoffs."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking orders on insufficient stock prevents overselling (which causes refunds, compensation, and customer churn). However, false blocks due to stale data or overly conservative availability calculations directly lose revenue. The definition of 'Available Stock' has direct financial consequences.","operational":"Introduces a synchronous or near-synchronous dependency between two previously independent systems. Warehouse operations (receiving, picking, adjustments) now directly affect order acceptance latency. Warehouse system downtime now blocks order placement — a significant operational risk that did not exist before.","user":"Sales team gains visibility into stock, reducing wasted effort on unfulfillable orders. However, 'real-time' creates false confidence — sales reps may treat the indicator as a guarantee and be frustrated when orders are still blocked. Customer experience depends heavily on how 'blocked' orders are communicated.","risk":"HIGH. This integration creates tight coupling between two bounded contexts that were deliberately separated. The greatest risks are: (1) undefined 'Available Stock' formula leading to inconsistent behavior, (2) no reservation mechanism leading to oversell under concurrency, (3) cascading failures when the Warehouse context is unavailable, (4) the term 'real-time' setting unrealistic expectations.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Orders may be rejected at placement time instead of accepted and later cancelled. Better if communicated well; worse if the block is opaque or overly aggressive."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains stock visibility (positive) but must understand the indicator is approximate. Workflow changes: previously all orders were accepted; now some are blocked, requiring a new process for handling blocked orders."},{"group":"Warehouse Operations","impact":"Warehouse system now has a direct consumer (Sales) reading stock in near-real-time. Any latency, downtime, or data quality issue in the warehouse directly impacts order flow. Increased operational pressure on warehouse data accuracy."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"New category of inquiries: 'Why was my order blocked?' Support needs training on the new flow and access to stock/order status."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue","impact":"Reduced refunds from overselling (positive). Potential lost revenue from false blocks (negative). Net impact depends on Available Stock formula accuracy."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains multiple critical ambiguities and at least one near-contradiction. The terms 'block,' 'stock available,' and 'real-time' are undefined. There is no specification of a reservation/allocation mechanism, which is essential to prevent oversell under concurrency. The interaction model between contexts (synchronous call, event-driven, shared data) is unspecified. The partial vs. atomic order acceptance policy is missing. Acceptance criteria cannot be reliably defined until these ambiguities are resolved.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 invariant is clear and well-defined","The oversell prevention requirement under concurrency is a hard domain truth","The 'real-time' indicator will be eventually consistent at best — this is a physical constraint of warehouse operations"],"low_confidence_areas":["Definition of 'Available Stock' (on-hand? minus allocated? minus in-transit orders?)","Meaning of 'block' (reject, hold, queue)","Partial vs. all-or-nothing order blocking when only some lines are insufficient","Failure mode when Warehouse context is unavailable","Whether a reservation/allocation mechanism is intended"],"assumptions":["The two bounded contexts are deployed as separate services or modules with no shared database","'Real-time' is a business aspiration, not a hard latency SLA","The feature intends to prevent overselling, not merely inform"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The term 'Available Stock' is not defined. It could mean: (A) raw StockLevel (on-hand), (B) StockLevel minus already-allocated-to-open-orders, or (C) StockLevel minus allocated plus expected inbound supply (ATP — Available to Promise). Each definition produces fundamentally different blocking behavior. This is the single most important decision in this feature.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Customers","Warehouse Operations","Finance"],"options":["(A) Use raw StockLevel — simple but allows oversell when multiple orders are in flight","(B) StockLevel minus allocated — requires a Reservation/Allocation concept to be introduced","(C) ATP (Available to Promise) — most sophisticated, accounts for inbound supply, but adds significant complexity"],"recommendation":"Option B. Introduce a Reservation/Allocation concept. When an order passes the stock check, decrement Available Stock by the reserved amount. This prevents oversell without the complexity of ATP. This requires a new domain concept owned by either a new Inventory Allocation context or as an extension of Warehouse Management."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Block' is undefined. Does blocking mean: (A) the order is rejected and the customer must retry, (B) the order is accepted but held in a 'Pending Stock' state for manual review, or (C) the order is queued and automatically released when stock becomes available?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Customer Support"],"options":["(A) Reject — simplest, but worst customer experience","(B) Hold for review — balanced, but requires a new order state and workflow","(C) Auto-queue — best customer experience, but adds backorder complexity"],"recommendation":"Option B. Introduce an 'AwaitingStock' order status. This gives the sales team visibility and control without losing the customer's order. It also avoids the complexity of automated backorder management."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partial vs. all-or-nothing: when an Order has multiple OrderLines and only some Products have insufficient stock, should the entire order be blocked or only the insufficient lines?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Operations"],"options":["(A) All-or-nothing — entire order blocked if any line is insufficient","(B) Partial — block only insufficient lines, accept the rest","(C) Customer choice — present the options and let the customer decide"],"recommendation":"Option A for initial implementation. All-or-nothing is simpler, preserves order integrity, and avoids complex partial-shipment logic. Can evolve to Option C later."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Failure mode when Warehouse context is unavailable: should Sales fail-closed (reject all orders) or fail-open (accept all orders optimistically)?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Finance"],"options":["(A) Fail-closed — no orders accepted, zero oversell risk, but revenue stops","(B) Fail-open — orders accepted without stock check, revenue continues, oversell risk returns","(C) Degrade gracefully — accept orders but flag them for post-hoc stock verification"],"recommendation":"Option C. Accept orders with a 'StockUnverified' flag. Run a reconciliation process when Warehouse context recovers. This balances revenue continuity with risk awareness."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The spec says 'Orders are placed by customers without checking Context A' (current behavior) but the new feature says 'automatically block if stock is insufficient.' These directly contradict. The feature is requesting a fundamental change to the order placement invariant — moving from 'accept all orders' to 'conditionally accept orders.' This is not a minor enhancement; it changes the core domain model of the Sales context. The existing invariant that orders are always accepted must be explicitly retired.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Customers","Warehouse Operations","Engineering"],"options":["(A) Retire the old behavior entirely — all orders now require stock validation","(B) Make stock validation optional/configurable per sales channel","(C) Clarify that the old description was the 'as-is' and the feature is the desired 'to-be'"],"recommendation":"Option C most likely, but this must be confirmed. If Option A, the entire Sales context contract changes and all consumers of the order placement API must be updated."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"No definition of 'Available Stock' — the core concept of the feature is undefined","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: Stock sufficiency must use a well-defined availability concept"},{"violation":"No concurrency protection mechanism specified — concurrent orders can oversell","business_rule_broken":"BR-4: Concurrent orders must not both succeed if combined quantity exceeds available stock"},{"violation":"'Block' behavior is undefined — cannot implement or test what isn't specified","business_rule_broken":"Cannot define acceptance criteria without knowing what 'blocked' means"},{"violation":"'Real-time' stock indicator has no defined staleness tolerance or accuracy guarantee","business_rule_broken":"BR-5: Indicator must have a defined staleness threshold and communicate its approximate nature"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Integration of two previously independent bounded contexts — Warehouse Management (stock) and Sales/Order Management (orders) — to enforce stock sufficiency at order placement time and expose real-time stock visibility to the sales team. This is a classic cross-context coupling challenge in logistics and retail domains.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. Neither context currently owns this
